items.reality: Scaling fixes fairness for distance- and gradient-based methods; tree models on raw numerics often see no gain, and bad scaling choices (wrong fit scope, MinMax with outliers) can hurt.
items.example: A gradient-boosted tree on raw census features may match accuracy with or without StandardScaler because splits depend on order, not absolute units.

items.reality: It re-expresses values in standard-deviation units; extremes remain large in z-space and still influence mean and σ when fit.
items.example: A billion-dollar revenue row still has |z| ≫ 3 after StandardScaler if it was present when μ and σ were computed.

items.myth: Normalization and standardization mean the same thing in every library.
items.reality: Colloquially both mean 'rescaling features,' but StandardScaler standardizes (z-scores) while MinMax normalizes to a bounded range; papers and APIs use the terms inconsistently—check the formula.
items.example: Some teams say 'normalize' but implement MinMax to [0,1]; others mean zero-mean unit-variance StandardScaler.
